summarization.a: shows a DFT-calculated side view of an aniline molecule adsorbed flat on a metal surface, highlighting the atomic positions (C, N, H) relative to the top-layer metal atoms.
summarization.b: provides a top view of the same adsorption geometry, showing how the aromatic ring aligns with the metal lattice, illustrating adsorption orientation and registry.
summarization.c: displays only the top layer metal atoms extracted from the original DFT structure to create a simplified 2D surface grid for RSA modeling.
summarization.d: shows overlapping van der Waals spheres of each atom (C, N, H), whose union forms the molecular footprint used to determine steric blocking for RSA adsorbate packing simulations.
summarization.e: presents the final 2D molecular footprint shape, derived from atomic overlaps, which represents the exclusion zone an aniline molecule occupies on the surface.
summarization.f: shows a Random Sequential Adsorption (RSA) simulation, where multiple aniline footprints are placed across the surface, demonstrating molecular crowding and surface coverage behavior.

d.question: Why is the final footprint (panel e) important for modeling adsorption behavior on metal surfaces?
d.answer: The footprint defines how much surface area each aniline molecule occupies, controlling steric blocking during adsorption. In RSA simulations, this footprint determines the maximum achievable surface coverage and molecular arrangement, allowing realistic modeling of adsorption density and spatial distribution as shown in panel (f).

b.question: How does the representation in panel (d) differ from the molecular depictions in panels (a–c)?
b.answer: Panel (d) replaces atomic models with van der Waals disks, whereas panels (a–c) use ball-and-stick structural representations.

a.question: How is the adsorption footprint in panel (e) generated from the molecular structure shown in panels (a–d)?
a.answer: Yes — the footprint is generated by combining van der Waals disks of each atom to form a single union shape.

c.question: What structural feature of the aniline molecule determines the total exclusion area used in the RSA simulations?
c.answer: N atom van der Waals radius, C atom van der Waals radii, H atom van der Waals radii, Molecular orientation on the surface
